quote: Perfection is achieved, not when there is nothing more to add, but when there is nothing left to take away.
through_line: The tools winning the AI era aren't necessarily the most expressive — they're the most legible. TypeScript wins because AI can reason about its types; React wins because AI was trained on it. This structural logic reaches further than code: the same instinct that helps you see which technology to trust can help you see which commitments are worth holding, and which are being kept out of inertia rather than belief.

articles.title: AI chatbots and digital companions are reshaping emotional connection
articles.pitch: The American Psychological Association's flagship publication takes stock of what AI companions are doing to the human capacity for intimacy — not just loneliness metrics, but the more insidious shift in what people expect from each other. AI is always validating, always available, never argumentative; real relationships can't compete on those terms, and psychologists are beginning to worry that's the point.

articles.title: TypeScript's rise in the AI era: Insights from Lead Architect, Anders Hejlsberg
articles.pitch: The creator of TypeScript explains, in his own words, why the language is thriving precisely when AI is doing more of the coding — not despite type safety, but because of it. Ninety-four percent of errors generated by AI coding assistants are type-related; TypeScript catches them at compile time. A rare chance to hear the structural thinking behind a language from the person who designed it, at the moment that design is most being tested.

articles.title: Web Development in 2025: AI's React Bias vs. Native Web
articles.pitch: A sharp analysis of the feedback loop reshaping frontend development: AI was trained on millions of React repositories, so it generates React by default, which drives more React adoption, which feeds more training data. The Matthew Effect in code. The piece also asks whether this bias is calcifying into a structural disadvantage for the open web — and whether any alternative has a chance against that compounding advantage.

articles.title: The game-changing magic of knowing when to quit
articles.pitch: A lucid interview with Annie Duke — poker champion turned decision researcher — on why strategic quitting is a skill, not a failure. She makes the case for "kill criteria": conditions defined in advance that signal when a goal is no longer worth the cost of pursuing. The argument that separates her from standard quit-or-persist advice is structural: sunk costs aren't just an irrational bias, they're an ego trap that makes it hard to ask the clean question — would you start this today if you hadn't already started?

articles.title: Seeing Ourselves Clearly
articles.pitch: A Buddhist teacher on the practice of meeting yourself without the usual defenses — not as a project of self-improvement, but as a form of honesty that precedes any genuine change. The piece distinguishes between self-observation as a tool for growth and the subtler trap of using self-awareness as another way to manage how you appear, even to yourself.

articles.title: The Creative Intuition Of Frank Gehry
articles.pitch: Written shortly after Gehry's death at 96, this is Nathan Gardels's portrait of how one of the most original architectural minds of the twentieth century actually worked — not from theory, but from intuition, improvisation, and what Gehry himself called "not knowing." He described himself as "a vacuum cleaner, not a theorist." For anyone thinking about the relationship between vision, taste, and the willingness to make something that might fail publicly, this is an unexpectedly useful read.
